pattern: Since the surviving characters don't need to have been adjacent in the original string -- deleting everything else automatically makes every remaining copy of one letter sit next to each other -- the whole problem reduces to picking a single letter to keep and paying to delete every other occurrence, so the cheapest plan is just total cost minus the most expensive letter's total.
hints: The final string only has to consist of one repeated letter -- it does not need to be a leftover contiguous run from the original string, since removing everything else pulls the survivors together automatically.
That means you get to pick exactly one letter to keep entirely, and you must delete every occurrence of every other letter, no matter where they sit in the string.

For each of the 26 possible letters, add up the cost of every position that does NOT hold that letter -- that's the price of keeping only that letter. The cheapest of those 26 totals is the answer, and it's the same as subtracting the priciest single letter's own total cost from the cost of the whole string.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, the priciest letter is kept even though it appears just once
statement example, three distinct single-occurrence letters
statement example, already all one letter, nothing to delete
single character, nothing can or needs to be deleted
the two most expensive letters are non-adjacent occurrences of the same letter, scattered apart by another letter in between
a cheap letter dominates by count but an expensive rare letter still isn't worth keeping once its rarity is weighed against the majority's total cost
every position has equal cost so the letter with the most occurrences wins by count alone

stress.description: random lowercase strings with random costs across the constraint's value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('abcde') for _ in range(n))
    cost = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [s, cost]
